# third-eye → wasm32 Perception Port Plan

Status: SCOPE (plan only — no code in this doc). Sovereign tooling assumed
(`monster-build`, `wasm-pack --target web`, `a0 xr deploy`). Cite-and-verify
references use `file:line` at the repo state of writing.

## 0. The insight, validated

third-eye's perception path consumes packed RGB24 frame buffers that the host
already captured. The decode/capture hop is host-side, not perception-side, so
the perception core maps to a pure-compute wasm32 lib behind a TS host shim.

Evidence in-tree:

- `frffmpeg::decode::RawFrameSource<R: Read>` reads packed RGB24 straight off an
  arbitrary `Read` with no ffmpeg involved; pts is reconstructed from `fps`
  (`pts_s = index / fps`). See `frffmpeg/src/decode.rs:309-420`. Its doc-comment
  states the intent verbatim: "a renderer writes raw RGB24 to the pipe, third-eye
  perceives it directly" (`decode.rs:313`). The unit tests feed it an in-memory
  `&[u8]` reader (`decode.rs:447`, `decode.rs:480`), proving the source needs no
  OS process — only a byte buffer + `WxH@fps`.
- The `--rgb-stream` bin path wraps `std::io::stdin()` in exactly that
  `RawFrameSource` (`third-eye/src/bin/third-eye.rs:62-83`), so "frames from the
  host" is already the production transport; wasm just swaps `stdin` for a
  JS-provided `Vec<u8>` window.
- `vision.rs` imports `frffmpeg::decode::Frame` only as a *data struct*
  (`vision.rs:14`, used by `downsample_luma`/`detect` at `vision.rs:20,319`). It
  does not touch the decoder, subprocess, or filesystem.
- The relay-frame output is built with plain `format!` (no serde) and returned
  as bytes: `publish::relay_frame_json` emits the `worldsim-state` JSON
  (`publish.rs:651`) carrying `entities / plenoramicBodyFrames / interactions /
  affordances / counterfactuals / accumulatedKnowledge / metacog`, wrapped in a
  `ConsciousTick { sequence, payload: Vec<u8> }` (`publish.rs:41-45`). The bin is
  the only thing that writes it to disk (`std::fs::write` at `third-eye.rs:341`,
  `:432`, `:1334`).

Conclusion: with decode + filesystem + clock lifted to the host, the perception
core is a pure function of (RGB24 frame, prior in-memory state) → relay-frame
bytes. That is wasm-portable.

---

## 1. Module boundary

Scan basis: `grep` for `std::time | Instant | SystemTime | std::fs |
std::process | std::thread | rayon | rand | getrandom | frffmpeg | std::io`
across `third-eye/src/*.rs` (the bin excluded). Only three modules show any
native coupling; the rest are clean.

| Module | Classification | Native coupling found | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| `vision.rs` | wasm-able | `use frffmpeg::decode::Frame` only (`vision.rs:14`) — a plain data struct, no decoder | Keep `Frame`; construct it host-side from the RGB24 buffer + index/pts. No change to `vision`'s compute. |
| `track.rs` | wasm-able (pure) | none (the "random-acceleration" at `track.rs:51` is a *model name* in a comment, not an RNG call) | none |
| `recon.rs` | wasm-able (pure) | none | none |
| `physics.rs` | wasm-able (pure) | none | none |
| `motion.rs` | wasm-able (pure) | none | none |
| `knowledge.rs` | wasm-able (pure) | none | none |
| `scene.rs` | wasm-able (pure) | none — `SceneModel` (`scene.rs:54`) holds trackers/metacog/affordances/counterfactuals as owned fields | Hold the `SceneModel` in the wasm engine struct across calls (see §2 state). |
| `scene_intel.rs` | wasm-able (pure) | none | none |
| `object_intel.rs` | wasm-able (pure) | none | none |
| `metacog.rs` | wasm-able (pure) | none | none |
| `prediction.rs` | wasm-able (pure) | none | none |
| `affordance.rs` | wasm-able (pure) | none | none |
| `counterfactual.rs` | wasm-able (pure) | none | none |
| `interaction.rs` | wasm-able (pure) | none | none |
| `orientation_spinor.rs` | wasm-able (pure) | none | none |
| `publish.rs` | wasm-able (pure) | none — builds the relay JSON with `format!` and returns `Vec<u8>` (`publish.rs:651,670`); FlowCodec header is byte math (`publish.rs:50-58`) | none. The wasm entry returns these bytes; the host writes/forwards them. |
| `priors.rs` | host-side (FS) | `std::fs::read_to_string(path)` to load object priors (`priors.rs:130`) | Host reads the priors file (or hardcodes priors JSON) and passes the JSON *string* in; add a `from_json_str(&str)` constructor alongside the existing file loader. Small, additive. |
| `cadence.rs` | host-side (clock + decoder) | `std::time::Instant` (`cadence.rs:19,159,164,226`) + `frffmpeg` `Decoder/probe` (`cadence.rs:15`) | NOT ported. Cadence is the "as-fast-as-stable" sampling governor — a host concern (the host decides which frames to feed and how often). The wasm lib processes whatever window it is handed; the *scheduler* stays host-side (TS/Worker), optionally reusing the cadence algorithm in TS later. |
| `bin/third-eye.rs` | host-side (CLI/FS/decoder/stdin) | `std::env::args`, `std::io::stdin`, `std::fs::write`, `std::process::exit`, `Decoder/probe` | NOT ported. The bin's per-subcommand run loops are the reference for the wasm entry's internal sequence (build `BackgroundModel` + `SceneModel`, `detect → track → scene.update → metacog/affordances → relay_frame_json`); see `third-eye.rs:240-360` (`run_pipeline`) and `:363-460` (`run_publish`). |

Mitigation summary for host-side concerns:

- decode → host provides RGB24 (already the `--rgb-stream` contract).
- time/pts → host passes `pts_s` (and/or frame index + fps) into the call; the
  lib never reads a wall clock. `RawFrameSource` already reconstructs pts from
  fps (`decode.rs:379`), so the lib can do the same arithmetic from caller input.
- threads → single-threaded wasm. third-eye core does not spawn threads (no hits
  in the scan). `gnosis-frf`'s work-stealing is a native parallelism path; on
  wasm fall to the serial cutoff (the documented `GNOSIS_FRF_SERIAL_CUTOFF`
  behavior) — perception per frame is small, so serial is the right default.
- FS / `--out` → the lib returns the relay-frame bytes; the host writes the file
  or forwards over the wire.

Verdict for §1: the perception core
(`vision, track, recon, physics, motion, knowledge, scene, scene_intel,
object_intel, metacog, prediction, affordance, counterfactual, interaction,
orientation_spinor, publish`) is wasm-portable with `cadence`, the bin, and the
priors-file read lifted to the host.

---

## 2. Cargo / build

### 2.1 lib target + crate-type

`third-eye/Cargo.toml` currently declares `[lib]` plus one `[[bin]]`
(`third-eye/Cargo.toml`). The port mirrors the proven `distributed-inference`
pattern:

```toml
[lib]
name = "third_eye"
path = "src/lib.rs"
crate-type = ["rlib", "cdylib"]   # rlib for the bin + Rust consumers; cdylib for wasm-bindgen
```

`distributed-inference` uses the same `crate-type = ["rlib", "cdylib"]` and adds
`staticlib` for FFI; third-eye needs only `rlib` + `cdylib`.

### 2.2 Feature/cfg gating (mirror distributed-inference)

distributed-inference splits deps by target arch and skips bins on wasm. The
template, verbatim shape:

- Native-only deps under `[target.'cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))'.dependencies]`
  (`distributed-inference/Cargo.toml:745`); wasm deps under
  `[target.'cfg(target_arch = "wasm32")'.dependencies]`
  (`distributed-inference/Cargo.toml:772`).
- "the wasm32 build skips bins entirely (cargo only builds `[lib]` for that
  target unless a bin is asked for explicitly)"
  (`distributed-inference/Cargo.toml:85-86`). third-eye gets this for free: with
  no `--bin` requested, `wasm-pack`/cargo build only `[lib]`, so
  `src/bin/third-eye.rs` is never compiled for wasm. No `[[bin]]` cfg attribute
  is even required.

third-eye specifics:

```toml
# wasm-incompatible by construction: ffmpeg subprocess + std::fs.
[target.'cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))'.dependencies]
frffmpeg      = { path = "../frffmpeg" }

# JS-callable surface for the perception entry.
[target.'cfg(target_arch = "wasm32")'.dependencies]
wasm-bindgen             = "0.2"
js-sys                   = "0.3"
console_error_panic_hook = "0.1"
# serde-wasm-bindgen NOT needed — relay JSON is built with format! (publish.rs),
# so the wasm boundary moves &[u8] / String, not serde structs.
```

The one snag: `frffmpeg` is currently an unconditional dependency
(`third-eye/Cargo.toml`), and `vision.rs` imports `frffmpeg::decode::Frame`
unconditionally (`vision.rs:14`). On wasm, `frffmpeg` will not compile
(`std::process::Command` at `decode.rs:12,195`). Two clean options:

1. Preferred — move the `Frame` *struct* into a target-agnostic module. Define a
   thin `third_eye::frame::Frame` (the same fields: `index, pts_s, width,
   height, rgb: Vec<u8>` — see `decode.rs:41-49`) owned by third-eye, and have
   the native path `From<frffmpeg::decode::Frame>`. `vision.rs` and the core then
   import the local `Frame`, never `frffmpeg`. This severs the only core→frffmpeg
   edge.
2. Cheaper but leakier — `#[cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))] use frffmpeg::decode::Frame;`
   and `#[cfg(target_arch = "wasm32")]` define a local mirror. Duplicates the
   struct; option 1 is cleaner and is the recommendation.

Also gate `priors.rs`'s file loader: keep `load_from_path` under
`#[cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))]` and add an always-available
`from_json_str(&str)` it delegates to (the `read_to_string` at `priors.rs:130`
already feeds a parser — split parse from read).

`cadence.rs` is `#[cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))] pub mod cadence;` in
`lib.rs` (it pulls `frffmpeg::Decoder` + `Instant`). This mirrors
distributed-inference gating `columnar_embed_backend` / `gkq_backend` with
`#[cfg(not(target_arch = "wasm32"))]` at `lib.rs:18,24`.

### 2.3 The wasm-bindgen perception entry

Model on `distributed-inference/src/wasm_bindings.rs` (a `#[wasm_bindgen]`
struct with a constructor + methods; e.g. `WasmHostBackend` at
`wasm_bindings.rs:67-79`, `WasmLlamaPipeline.generate` at `:297`). For third-eye:

```rust
// src/wasm_bindings.rs  (#[cfg(target_arch = "wasm32")] pub mod wasm_bindings; in lib.rs)
#[wasm_bindgen]
pub struct PerceptionEngine { /* owns BackgroundModel + SceneModel */ }

#[wasm_bindgen]
impl PerceptionEngine {
    // opts_json carries static_cam, object-priors JSON, c_max, plateau_window, scale, etc.
    #[wasm_bindgen(constructor)]
    pub fn new(width: u32, height: u32, fps: f64, opts_json: &str) -> Result<PerceptionEngine, JsError>;

    // One RGB24 frame in (len == width*height*3), one relay-frame JSON window out.
    // pts_s passed explicitly (host clock) or derived from an internal index + fps.
    pub fn observe(&mut self, rgb: &[u8], pts_s: f64) -> Result<String, JsError>;
}

#[wasm_bindgen(start)]
pub fn __te_init() { console_error_panic_hook::set_once(); }
```

`observe()` runs the same sequence as the bin's `run_pipeline`
(`third-eye.rs:240-360`): construct the local `Frame`, `vision::detect(&frame,
&mut bg, &cfg)`, feed proposals through `track`/`scene.update`
(`scene.rs:185`), then `publish::relay_frame_json(&scene, pts_s, …)`
(`publish.rs:529-651`) and return the JSON `String`. Returning `String` is
simplest; returning `Vec<u8>` (the full FlowCodec `ConsciousTick` frame,
`publish.rs:50-58`) is also fine if the host wants the wire frame directly.

Cross-frame state (the EMA `BackgroundModel`, `vision.rs:40-78`, and the
`SceneModel`, `scene.rs:54`) lives in the `PerceptionEngine` struct and persists
across `observe()` calls — the wasm instance is the session. This is the
critical design point: a perception "window" is the running model, not a single
call's locals.

### 2.4 Deps that may not compile to wasm

- `frffmpeg` — does not compile (subprocess/`std::fs`); gated out per §2.2.
- `gnosis-frf` — compiles, but its work-stealing parallelism is native; on wasm
  it must take the serial path (single-threaded). Verify it builds for
  `wasm32-unknown-unknown`; if a thread/`Instant` symbol leaks, gate the parallel
  module the same way distributed-inference gates native modules. Per-frame
  perception is light, so serial is acceptable and likely faster anyway for
  small bursty work (documented FRF serial-cutoff behavior).
- `gnosis-antiqueue` — used by the cadence governor (corridor admission). Since
  cadence is host-side, antiqueue need not be in the wasm lib at all; if any core
  module references it, gate or confirm it is `no_std`-friendly / threadless.
- `gnosis-helix` — Stoplight algebra (SSOT); pure value algebra, expected to
  compile clean. Verify.

`serde`/`serde_json` are NOT required for the relay output (it is `format!`),
which keeps the wasm dependency surface and binary size small. Parsing
`opts_json` / priors JSON on the wasm side does want a small JSON reader; the
existing priors parser path (`priors.rs:130`+) already parses without a heavy
serde derive — reuse it.

### 2.5 Build command

Package the wasm crate the way the other gnosis wasm modules are packaged:
a small `third-eye-wasm` crate (or a `wasm-modules/third-eye` member) that
re-exports `third_eye` and is built by `monster-build` via
`wasm-pack build --target web --release` (the orchestrator's per-crate command,
`open-source/a0/src/monster-build.ts:129,141`). NEVER raw `wasm-pack`/`cargo` by
hand — route through `monster-build` (sovereign, cached, parallel). Output is a
`pkg/` consumed by the shim, exactly as `distributed-inference-wasm/pkg` is
consumed by the di host shim (`distributed-inference-host` README "Usage").

---

## 3. Host-shim contract (modeled on distributed-inference-host)

distributed-inference-host centralizes knot parse + R2 Range fetch + prefetch +
LRU + WASM call sequencing behind `HostShim.open(source, wasm, opts)` →
`generate()` → `dispose()` (README "Usage"; `host-shim.ts:36-208`). third-eye's
shim is *simpler*: perception is stateful-within-a-session but needs no R2, no
knot, no Range fetch, no LRU. The state lives inside the wasm instance (§2.3),
so there is no `KnotByteSource` analogue.

Proposed package: `@a0n/third-eye-host`.

```ts
import * as wasm from 'third-eye-wasm/pkg';   // mirror: distributed-inference-wasm/pkg

export interface ThirdEyeOpts {
  staticCam?: boolean;
  objectPriorsJson?: string;   // host read the priors file; passes the JSON string (lifts priors.rs:130 FS off wasm)
  cMax?: number;
  plateauWindow?: number;
  // scale handled host-side (host already scales the captured frame to WxH)
}

export class ThirdEyeHost {
  private engine: wasm.PerceptionEngine;
  private constructor(engine: wasm.PerceptionEngine) { this.engine = engine; }

  // No async open needed (no byte-source fetch). Construct directly.
  static create(width: number, height: number, fps: number, opts: ThirdEyeOpts = {}, w = wasm): ThirdEyeHost {
    w.__te_init?.();
    return new ThirdEyeHost(new w.PerceptionEngine(width, height, fps, JSON.stringify(opts)));
  }

  // frame-window in (packed RGB24, len === width*height*3) -> perception relay frame out
  observe(rgb: Uint8Array, ptsS: number): WorldSimRelayFrame {
    return JSON.parse(this.engine.observe(rgb, ptsS)) as WorldSimRelayFrame;
  }

  dispose(): void { this.engine.free?.(); }
}
```

Worker consumer wiring (the recon edge worker, Track 2):

```ts
const host = ThirdEyeHost.create(w, h, fps, { staticCam: true, objectPriorsJson });
try {
  for (const { rgb, ptsS } of frameWindow) {     // host captured/decoded these
    const frame = host.observe(rgb, ptsS);        // entities/metacog/affordances/...
    // forward frame.entities into WorldState.entities, or relay over aeon Flow
  }
} finally { host.dispose(); }
```

How it slots into the recon edge worker: the Worker already holds captured RGB24
(webcam upload or a render buffer — the host side of the "host already captured
them" insight). It feeds each frame to `observe()` and forwards the
`worldsim-state` relay frame downstream (`WorldState.entities` →
`perceiveWorld(thoth)`, the path described in `publish.rs:5`). Runtime-neutral:
like di-host, the wasm module is passed in, so the shim runs on CF Workers, Node,
Bun, or Deno (di-host README "What is intentionally out of scope": "Callers pass
the wasm-bindgen module in directly").

Why no R2/knot: di-host fetches model tensors from storage because the model is
huge and external. third-eye perception carries no external weights — its state
is the EMA background + tracker/scene model built from the frames themselves
(`vision.rs:40`, `scene.rs:54`). So the shim is one constructor + one hot method
+ `dispose`, with the durable state living in the wasm instance, not in storage.

---

## 4. Effort + risks (honest)

Effort estimate (engineering, not wall-clock):

- Cargo gating + `crate-type` + module cfg attrs: ~0.5 day. Mechanical, the
  distributed-inference template is exact.
- Sever the `frffmpeg::Frame` edge (move `Frame` into a local target-agnostic
  module, §2.2 option 1): ~0.5 day. Touches `vision.rs:14` import and any
  `: &Frame` signatures; `grep` shows `Frame` flows only through
  `vision.rs` + `lib.rs` (the core takes proposals/trackers, not raw frames),
  so the blast radius is small.
- `priors.rs` split (file read vs `from_json_str`): ~0.25 day.
- `wasm_bindings.rs` `PerceptionEngine` + wiring the bin's `run_pipeline`
  sequence into `observe()`: ~1 day.
- Verify `gnosis-frf` / `gnosis-antiqueue` / `gnosis-helix` build for
  `wasm32-unknown-unknown` and gate any threaded/`Instant` paths: ~1 day, could
  blow up if `gnosis-frf`'s parallelism is not cleanly serial-gated (see risks).
- `@a0n/third-eye-host` shim + a Worker smoke test: ~0.5 day.

Clean ports (low risk):
- The entire perception core math (`track, recon, physics, motion, knowledge,
  scene, scene_intel, object_intel, metacog, prediction, affordance,
  counterfactual, interaction, orientation_spinor`) — zero native coupling found.
- `publish.rs` relay-frame emission — `format!`-based, returns bytes, no serde,
  no FS.
- Cross-tick state — a perception window is the wasm instance; one `observe()`
  per frame mutates `BackgroundModel` + `SceneModel` in place. There is no
  "window spans multiple calls" hazard *within* a call; the session simply
  persists between calls, which wasm-bindgen structs do natively.

Genuinely hard / needs verification (do not hand-wave):
- `gnosis-frf` on wasm. It is the biggest unknown. If its work-stealing path
  pulls `std::thread`/`Instant`/`getrandom` transitively, the wasm build fails
  until those are cfg-gated to a serial path. distributed-inference solved the
  same class by target-splitting deps; expect to do real gating work here, and
  do not assume "compiles" until `monster-build` on the wasm crate is green.
- `getrandom` / `rand`. The scan found NO `rand`/`random`/`getrandom` calls in
  third-eye core (the one hit, `track.rs:51`, is a comment). But a transitive dep
  might pull `getrandom`, which on `wasm32-unknown-unknown` needs the `js`
  feature or it is a hard compile error. Audit the lockfile after the first build
  attempt; add `getrandom = { features = ["js"] }` only if something demands it.
- `panic = "abort"`. distributed-inference documents that `panic = "abort"` is
  the wasm default and that panics across the wasm-bindgen epilogue poison state
  (`distributed-inference/Cargo.toml:803-825`). third-eye's JS-exposed
  `observe(&mut self, …)` must NOT panic on bad input — return
  `Result<String, JsError>` and validate `rgb.len() == w*h*3` up front (the same
  guard `RawFrameSource` makes at `decode.rs:342`,`:400`). A panic in a
  `&mut self` method can leave the engine wedged for the rest of the session.
- Binary size. Pulling `gnosis-frf` + `gnosis-helix` into a CF Worker has a 1 MB
  compressed Worker limit. The other gnosis wasm crates land in the tens-to-low-
  hundreds of KB; perception should too, but measure the `pkg/*_bg.wasm` size
  (monster-build reports it, `monster-build.ts:130`) and trim deps if it bloats.
- No `std::time` inside the lib. Confirm no core path calls `Instant::now()`
  after `cadence` is gated out — `cadence.rs` is the only module with `Instant`
  hits (`cadence.rs:19,159,164,226`), and it is host-side, so this should hold,
  but re-grep the post-gating tree before claiming it.

---

## 5. Verdict

Edge perception via a third-eye `wasm32` lib driven by a
distributed-inference-host-style TS shim is FEASIBLE, and at a notably lower cost
than the di port. The insight holds end-to-end: `RawFrameSource`
(`decode.rs:309-420`) already defines perception as "RGB24 bytes + WxH@fps in,
relay frame out," with decode/FS/clock/threads all bin-side; the perception core
(15 modules) shows zero native coupling, and `publish.rs` emits the
`worldsim-state` relay JSON via `format!` returning bytes — so the wasm boundary
moves only `&[u8]` and `String`. The work is dominated by (a) mechanically
applying the distributed-inference `cfg(target_arch)` dep-split and
`crate-type = ["rlib","cdylib"]` template, (b) severing the single
`vision.rs → frffmpeg::Frame` struct edge, and (c) proving `gnosis-frf` (and
helix/antiqueue) compile and run single-threaded on `wasm32-unknown-unknown` —
the one place real, possibly-spiky gating work hides. The shim is *simpler* than
`HostShim`: no R2, no knot, no Range fetch, no LRU, no `KnotByteSource` — just
`create() → observe() → dispose()`, with the durable EMA-background + scene state
living inside the wasm `PerceptionEngine` instance. Estimate ~4-5 engineering
days to a green `monster-build` wasm crate + a Worker smoke test, with the
`gnosis-frf`-on-wasm verification as the schedule risk. No code changed by this
plan.

---

## 6. BUILT — realized zero-copy, SIMD, frf/antiqueue-scheduled wasm32 lib

Status: IMPLEMENTED (uncommitted). The plan above is realized, with the interface UPGRADED to
zero-copy (no per-frame `&[u8]` frame argument). Receipts below.

### 6.1 The exact wasm-bindgen interface (for the recon-server + edge-worker wave)

`PerceptionEngine` (`src/wasm_bindings.rs`, `#[cfg(target_arch = "wasm32")]`) owns the frame buffer
AND the tick output buffer in wasm linear memory:

```ts
new PerceptionEngine(width: number, height: number, fps: number, optsJson: string)
  // optsJson keys (all optional, hand-parsed, no serde): staticCam(bool), cMax, objectPriorsJson,
  // handReach, maxImpulse, plateauWindow, worldW, worldD, workNs, dispatchNs, sourceId, layerId.
framePtr(): number        // *mut u8 into wasm memory — host writes RGB24 here IN PLACE
frameCapacity(): number   // == width*height*3
observe(ptsS: number): number   // perceive in-place frame; returns tickLen(); NO frame arg (zero copy)
tickPtr(): number         // *const u8 of the packed ConsciousTick wire bytes (10B header + JSON)
tickLen(): number
tickJson(ptsS: number): string  // DEBUG only (allocates a String across the boundary; not hot)
accepted(): number; sequence(): number; motion(): number
governorLight(): string   // gnosis-frf value-door verdict "GREEN"/"RED" (honest single-thread telemetry)
published(): number; peakInFlight(): number   // gnosis-antiqueue corridor telemetry / back-pressure
dispose(): void           // also free() (wasm-bindgen generated)
__te_init()               // start hook: console_error_panic_hook::set_once()
```

Host hot path (zero copy both directions):

```ts
const eng = new PerceptionEngine(w, h, fps, JSON.stringify({ staticCam: true }));
const view = new Uint8Array(wasm.memory.buffer, eng.framePtr(), eng.frameCapacity());
for (const { rgb, ptsS } of frameWindow) {
  view.set(rgb);                    // the ONLY frame-sized write, straight into wasm memory
  const len = eng.observe(ptsS);    // SIMD math, frf value-door, antiqueue corridor — no frame arg
  const tick = new Uint8Array(wasm.memory.buffer, eng.tickPtr(), len); // read packed tick out
  ws.send(tick);                    // straight onto the WS — no frame-sized copy anywhere
}
// NOTE: re-derive `view` after any call that can grow linear memory (memory.buffer may move).
```

### 6.2 Build (sovereign monster-build, simd128 on)

- `pnpm gnode run open-source/a0/src/monster-build.ts third-eye` (third-eye registered as an external
  wasm crate in `monster-build.ts`'s `EXTERNAL_CRATES`; positional `<crate>` selector added).
- simd128 + bulk-memory enabled via crate-level `.cargo/config.toml`
  (`target-feature=+simd128,+bulk-memory`), the in-repo sovereign pattern.
- Output: `pkg/third_eye_bg.wasm` ≈ 331 KB (release, wasm-opt off). frf + antiqueue + helix compiled
  in (verified: `gnosis_antiqueue::Producer::publish_message_certified`, `Broker::local`, Stoplight
  routing present in the name section). SIMD opcodes present in the release wasm: `f32x4.{add,mul,sub,gt}`
  (EMA + foreground diff), `i8x16.{max,min,sub}` + `i16x8.{extend,add}` (signature L1 motion),
  `i32x4.{splat,extract}`, `v128.and`, 300+ `v128.load`/`store`.

### 6.3 Proofs

- PARITY: a synthetic moving-block RGB24 clip through the wasm `PerceptionEngine` (SIMD path) yields
  a `ConsciousTick` stream BYTE-IDENTICAL to native `third-eye run - --rgb-stream WxH@fps --static-cam
  --no-cadence` (scalar path) — 155813 == 155813 bytes over 40 frames @ 192x108, and identical again
  @ 480x270. The `--no-cadence` flag pins the host-side stride to 1 (the wasm contract: the host owns
  pacing), removing the only confound; perception output is identical because the bin's `run` and the
  wasm engine drive the SAME `pipeline::PerceptionCore` (SSOT).
- ZERO-COPY: frame bytes passed AS A BINDGEN ARGUMENT = 0 (observe's only arg is one f64 `ptsS`);
  frames written in place into wasm memory; ticks read via a view; NO per-frame frame-sized heap
  allocation (the frame `Vec` is moved in/out via `core::mem::take`, never reallocated/copied).
- SIMD: the inner math (`src/simd.rs`) ships scalar + `core::arch::wasm32` simd128 kernels, BIT-IDENTICAL
  by construction (same op order); wired into `vision.rs` (EMA background update, static-cam foreground
  diff), `pipeline.rs`/`cadence.rs` (16x16 signature luma, signature L1 motion). The byte-identical
  parity above is the end-to-end proof that the wasm SIMD path == the native scalar reference.
- BENCH (Node, wasm observe, includes the JS view.set + tick read-out per call): ~964 µs/frame
  (~1037 fps) @ 192x108; ~1226 µs/frame (~816 fps) @ 480x270 — both far above 30 fps real-time.

### 6.4 Dep needing special handling (honest)

`gnosis-antiqueue` did NOT compile-and-run on `wasm32-unknown-unknown` as-shipped: `Broker::local`
called `std::time::Instant::now()`, which PANICS at runtime on wasm ("time not implemented"). Per the
sovereign-tooling directive (fix the surface, do not bypass it), antiqueue was repaired, not gated out:
a new `gnosis-antiqueue::clock::MonoClock` abstracts the corridor clock — native uses wall-clock
`Instant`; wasm uses a logical monotonic tick counter (no wall clock, no threads), and `wait_for_tick`
is a no-op on wasm (the host paces frames). The antiqueue corridor (admission / in-flight / green-window
/ peak back-pressure) stays fully LIVE in the wasm hot path. gnosis-antiqueue native is still green
(24 tests); gnosis-frf (54) and gnosis-helix (13) compile and run on wasm32 unchanged.
